evals.prompt:
I need to completely redesign my training platform from scratch. It's a React/Vite/TypeScript app that teaches manual testers to become automated testers using Playwright, VS Code, and GitHub Copilot. There are 27 modules right now but we're expanding to 30. The app needs to work like a scrolling webpage — not a slide deck, not paginated — a continuous scroll with text and code sections intertwined like a notebook.

I need:
- A left-hand navigation panel showing all 30 modules with completion state, the ability to expand each module to see its lessons, and clear visual indication of where the learner currently is
- A large center content pane that's the main learning area with the notebook-style scroll (text blocks, code blocks, exercises, quizzes all in one flow)
- A right panel for supplementary materials like glossary, notes, resources, and reference docs — collapsible so it doesn't crowd the main content
- A progress bar that shows overall course progress and also progress within the current module
- Easy back/forward navigation between lessons without losing scroll position
- Theme support — I want it to feel dynamic across the 30 modules, not the same look for every single one
- The design should be professional, clean, modern. Not overly technical or intimidating — remember these are manual testers learning to code for the first time. Spacing should be generous, text should breathe, code blocks should feel natural in the flow not bolted on.

Break this into phases. Don't try to do everything at once. I want to see:

Phase 1: Explore what exists in the codebase right now — what components, styles, routing, state do we already have?
Phase 2: Design the architecture — shell layout, theme strategy, component plan, navigation model. Show me the design spec before writing code.
Phase 3: Build the foundation — shell, theme tokens, routing, progress data model. Use subagents in parallel for independent pieces.
Phase 4: Build the component library — spin up parallel subagents for different component families (nav, content, interactive, layout).
Phase 5: Assemble pages from the components — one subagent per page type.
Phase 6: Audit everything with /design-framework and fix any issues.

Use subagents and tools throughout. Don't hold everything in one context — split the work up so each agent gets only the reference files it needs. Explore the codebase first, design second, build third, audit last.
evals.expected_output: Should execute in explicit phases with clear deliverables at each step. Phase 1: Explore agents scanning existing codebase, producing an inventory. Phase 2: Design spec document with shell architecture, theme cycling map, component plan, layout registry selections, navigation model — presented for user review before coding. Phase 3: Parallel subagents building shell layout, theme token system, routing structure, progress data model. Phase 4: Parallel subagents building component families (navigation, content, interactive, layout) — each getting only relevant reference files. Phase 5: Parallel subagents assembling page types from Phase 4 components. Phase 6: /design-framework audit on all pages, accessibility checks, theme cycling verification. Should use specific layout IDs from the registry, named themes (Signal Cobalt, Arctic Steel, Linear, Gamma Dark), training triangle concepts, and micro-cadence throughout. Should NOT try to do everything in a single pass.

evals.prompt:
Build the lesson page for Module 7 Lesson 3: 'Writing Your First Playwright Test.' It should explain page.goto() and page.click(), show code examples in a notebook-style scroll, then have an exercise where the learner writes a test against the practice-app's login page. Show a diff when they check their answer. This is an odd-numbered module so use a dark theme. Include progress tracking showing they're on lesson 3 of 8 in this module and module 7 of 30 overall.

Break it into steps: first explore what lesson page patterns exist, then design the layout, then build the content components and lesson shell in parallel using subagents, then assemble and audit.
evals.expected_output: Should work in phases: (1) Explore existing lesson page code, (2) Design layout using registry layouts, (3) Parallel subagents for lesson shell and code/diff components, (4) Assemble and audit. Should produce a lesson detail screen using dark theme (Signal Cobalt or Gamma Dark), notebook-style scroll, CodeBlock with Playwright syntax, exercise targeting practice-app login page, DiffView with gentle tone, progress indicators at module and course level, correct layout selection.

evals.prompt:
Design the Module 14 overview page for 'Page Object Model.' This is an even-numbered module so it should use a light theme. Show the 6 lessons in this module with completion state, estimated times, prerequisites (must complete Module 13 first), and a preview of what they'll build. The learner has completed 13 of 30 modules. The left nav should show their position in the full course.

Phase it: explore existing module overview code first, then design the layout spec, then build it, then audit with /design-framework.
evals.expected_output: Should work in phases: (1) Explore existing module overview code, (2) Design layout spec with registry layout IDs, (3) Build components, (4) Audit with /design-framework. Should produce module overview page using light theme (Arctic Steel or Linear), ModuleHero component, lesson list with completion tracking, prerequisite callout, progress visualization (13/30), left nav with current position, correct layout selection.

evals.prompt:
Create a quiz component that asks the learner to write a Playwright locator for a login button. It should accept their code input, compare it to the expected answer with a visual diff, and give gentle feedback like 'Almost — here's what's different' instead of marking it wrong. The quiz is embedded in a lesson scroll, not a separate page. Use whatever theme the current module uses.

First step: explore existing quiz components in the codebase so you don't duplicate what's there. Second step: design the component API. Third step: build it with /frontend-design. Fourth step: audit with /design-framework.
evals.expected_output: Should work in steps: (1) Explore existing quiz components, (2) Design component API/props, (3) Build with /frontend-design, (4) Audit with /design-framework. Should produce InteractiveCheck with code input via CodeBlock, DiffView for answer comparison with educational tone, gentle microcopy, accessibility (icons+labels not just color), embedded in notebook scroll, theme-aware tokens.
